title: CC ecommerce holds above POS at 50.2% of total CC volume
body: In Mar 2026, ecommerce accounted for 50.2% (+0.1pp vs prior month) of total CC transaction volume vs POS at 49.1%. This structural shift — digital-first over in-store — has persisted for multiple months.
implication: With ecommerce at 50.2% of CC volume, the majority of CC spend is now card-not-present (CNP). Lenders should review fraud scoring models calibrated on POS-dominant spend patterns — CNP transactions carry higher chargeback exposure and require digital-first decisioning.

title: Credit cards outstanding: 5 consecutive months of growth — 118.6M cards
body: Total credit cards outstanding reached 118.6M in Mar 2026 (+2.5% QoQ vs Q3 FY26), marking the 5 consecutive months of growth. Issuance momentum is broad-based across bank types.
implication: Consecutive months of CC card growth (5 months) indicates sustained acquisition momentum. Lenders should track activation rates alongside issuance — dormant card growth inflates headline numbers without building transaction-based credit history.

title: All CC transaction types surged in Mar 2026 — avg 15.8% MoM
body: All four CC transaction types grew strongly in Mar 2026: POS +16.7%, eCommerce +17.2%, ATM +11.4%, Other +18.1%. March year-end spending typically drives broad-based CC transaction growth.
implication: Year-end CC transaction surges (avg 15.8% MoM across all types) are seasonal — not reflective of structural demand growth. Underwriters should normalise Q4 spend volumes when assessing credit utilisation or limit adequacy to avoid overstating repayment capacity.

title: SFB banks gained CC card share in Mar 2026 (+0.1pp)
body: SFB banks hold 1.5% of total credit cards outstanding in Mar 2026 (+0.1pp vs prior month). Private banks lost the most share at -0.1pp, now at 71.0%. SFB growth in credit cards reflects increased fintech partnerships.
implication: SFB banks gaining 0.1pp CC share signals a shift in acquisition strategy or partnership activity in that segment. For lenders benchmarking portfolio quality, tracking which bank type is gaining share helps contextualise portfolio concentration risk and pricing expectations in the credit card market.

title: Top 5 banks hold 74.3% of CC cards — HDFC BANK LTD leads at 22.2%
body: In Mar 2026, the top 5 banks account for 74.3% of total credit cards outstanding (-0.2pp vs prior month). HDFC BANK LTD leads with 22.2% share (+0.2% MoM).
implication: With 74.3% concentration in the top 5 CC issuers, smaller banks have limited scale to build competitive rewards programs or co-brand partnerships. For credit strategy teams, this concentration means data from RBI's aggregate CC series is effectively proxied by a handful of banks' portfolios.

title: Gap: Foreign bank CC share at 3.7% (-0.0pp MoM) — premium segment shrinking
body: Foreign banks hold only 3.7% of total credit cards outstanding in Mar 2026, down 0.03pp vs prior month. Foreign banks traditionally serve the high-income, high-spend segment — their declining share signals continued loss of the premium CC market to private Indian banks.
implication: Foreign bank CC share at 3.7% and declining means the premium-card, high-limit borrower segment is consolidating within Indian private banks. For credit intelligence, the RBI CC data increasingly reflects domestic bank portfolios — foreign bank premium cardholder behaviour is underrepresented and requires separate data sources.

title: DC ATM cash losing share — 81.4% of DC volume (-0.5pp) as digital grows
body: Debit card ATM withdrawals account for 81.4% of total DC transaction volume in Mar 2026 (-0.5pp vs prior month). DC ecommerce has grown to 4.8% (+0.3pp). The structural shift from cash to digital payments is underway in the debit segment.
implication: DC ATM cash at 81.4% and declining creates an expanding segment of digitally-active debit customers with improving transaction footprints. Lenders with access to debit transaction data should segment on this shift — moving-to-digital debit customers are prime candidates for first-credit product origination.

title: DC POS cash withdrawals: 2 consecutive months of decline (-7.0% MoM)
body: Debit card POS cash-back withdrawal volume fell 7.0% MoM in Mar 2026, the 2 consecutive months of decline. This is a separate channel from ATM cash — POS cash-back usage is contracting while digital POS payments continue to grow.
implication: Declining POS cash-back withdrawals indicate merchants and customers are shifting toward net digital payments rather than using POS as a cash-out channel. This is a positive signal for POS-data quality — transaction records increasingly reflect actual purchases rather than cash access events, improving their value for credit underwriting.

title: DC ecommerce is 4.8% of total DC transaction volume in Mar 2026
body: Debit card ecommerce accounted for 4.8% of total DC transaction volume in Mar 2026 (+0.3pp vs prior month). ATM cash still dominates at 81.4%. The structural shift away from cash toward digital payments is ongoing.
implication: Debit card ecommerce at 4.8% of DC volume creates a digitally-traceable subpopulation of debit-only customers — higher-value prospects for credit product cross-sell. Lenders with access to debit transaction data should segment by digital vs ATM-cash behaviour to prioritise credit outreach.

title: Debit cards: 5 consecutive months of growth — 1.04B outstanding
body: Total debit cards outstanding reached 1.04B in Mar 2026 (+1.0% QoQ vs Q3 FY26), the 5 consecutive months of growth. India debit base continues to expand.
implication: India debit card base at 1.04B provides a large population with formal banking access. However, card count alone overstates addressable credit opportunity — lenders should focus on the active-transacting subset, which is the realistic pool for first-credit products.

title: PSB banks hold 62.9% of debit cards — PSB gaining share in Mar 2026
body: Public sector banks account for 62.9% of total debit cards outstanding in Mar 2026 (+0.1pp vs prior month). Private banks hold 25.1%. PSB banks are the fastest-growing category (+0.1pp share gain).
implication: PSB dominance at 62.9% of debit cards reflects Jan Dhan linkage and rural banking penetration rather than active acquisition. For credit lenders, PSB debit portfolios skew toward low-income, low-activity accounts — transaction-based underwriting signals must be calibrated separately from urban private bank debit portfolios.

title: STATE BANK OF INDIA leads DC cards at 24.2%
body: STATE BANK OF INDIA holds 24.2% of total debit cards in Mar 2026 (+0.5% MoM). Top 5 banks account for 49.8% (-0.1pp vs prior month).
implication: The top 5 DC banks hold 49.8% of debit cards. For lenders using debit card transaction data in underwriting, coverage quality is largely determined by data-sharing arrangements with these top banks — gaps in coverage create blind spots for credit decisions.

title: Gap: DC ATM cash at 81.4% of DC volume — digital transition is incomplete
body: Despite growth in digital payments, ATM cash withdrawals still account for 81.4% of total debit card transaction volume in Mar 2026 (-0.5pp vs prior month). DC ecommerce is only 4.8% of DC volume. India's debit card base remains overwhelmingly cash-dependent.
implication: With 81.4% of DC volume still being ATM cash, the majority of debit card transactions leave no digital footprint for underwriting purposes. Lenders building alternate data credit models on debit transaction data face severe coverage gaps — bureau supplementation remains essential for the cash-dominant debit segment.

title: Gap: DC ecommerce at 4.8% of DC volume — debit cards leave thin digital footprints
body: Debit card ecommerce transactions account for only 4.8% of total DC transaction volume in Mar 2026. ATM cash dominates at 81.4%. The vast majority of debit card holders transact primarily via ATM cash withdrawal, with minimal digital payment activity.
implication: DC ecommerce at 4.8% of volume means debit card transaction histories are predominantly cash events — not useful for digital underwriting. Lenders building first-credit products for the debit-primary mass market should rely on bureau + income-proxy signals rather than transaction-data models, which will suffer from severe data sparsity in this population.

title: India now has 65 UPI QR codes per POS terminal
body: As of Mar 2026, there are 65 UPI QR codes (761.2M) for every POS terminal (11.8M). This ratio was 65 in the prior month. UPI QR grew +0.9% MoM vs POS terminals at +0.9% MoM — digital acceptance infrastructure is outpacing hardware deployment.
implication: At 65 UPI QR codes per POS terminal, QR-based merchant acceptance has decisively outpaced hardware deployment. Lenders without credit products accessible via UPI QR (credit on UPI, BNPL on QR) are structurally excluded from the long-tail merchant base that has no POS terminal — this is the majority of small merchants.

title: POS terminals: 4 consecutive months of growth — 11.8M deployed
body: POS terminals reached 11.8M in Mar 2026 (+2.6% QoQ vs Q3 FY26), the 4 consecutive months of growth. Physical acceptance infrastructure continues to expand.
implication: Growing POS terminal count (11.8M and rising) signals merchant formalisation — merchants deploying POS terminals generate transaction histories that can underpin MSME credit assessments. Lenders with POS-linked lending products (merchant cash advances, working capital) benefit from this infrastructure growth.

title: UPI QR codes are 135x Bharat QR in scale — 761.2M vs 5.63M
body: As of Mar 2026, there are 761.2M UPI QR codes deployed vs 5.63M Bharat QR codes — a 135x gap. UPI QR grew +0.9% MoM vs Bharat QR at -4.5% MoM. UPI has decisively won the QR standard battle in India.
implication: UPI QR at 135x Bharat QR scale means any payments or lending infrastructure built on Bharat QR rails is operating on a de facto deprecated standard. Lenders evaluating QR-linked credit products should anchor on UPI QR as the only viable acceptance network at scale.

title: RBL BANK LTD leads POS terminal deployment at 22.5% market share
body: RBL BANK LTD has deployed 2.65M POS terminals in Mar 2026, accounting for 22.5% of total (+9.5% MoM). Top 5 banks hold 93.1% of all POS terminals (+0.1pp vs prior month). Notable: RBL BANK LTD moved from #2 to #1.
implication: Top 5 banks controlling 93.1% of POS terminals means merchant acquiring — and by extension, merchant transaction data — is concentrated in a handful of institutions. Lenders building merchant credit products outside these banks face significant data coverage gaps. Co-lending or data-sharing with top POS acquirers is a prerequisite for scale.

title: Gap: Bharat QR contracting -4.5% MoM — 5.63M vs 761.2M UPI QR
body: Bharat QR codes fell 4.5% MoM in Mar 2026, now at 5.63M — compared to 761.2M UPI QR codes (135x gap). Merchant preference has consolidated on UPI QR as the dominant QR acceptance standard.
implication: Bharat QR's -4.5% MoM decline signals the standard is being actively abandoned by merchants. Any lending or payments product built on Bharat QR infrastructure faces accelerating merchant disengagement. Reallocate merchant acquisition and credit-linked acceptance strategy to UPI QR rails exclusively.

title: Gap: Offsite ATMs declining -0.2% MoM — rural cash access contracting
body: Offsite ATMs fell 0.2% MoM in Mar 2026 (now 73.7K), while onsite ATMs grew +0.5% MoM. Offsite ATMs serve rural and semi-urban populations where branch presence is limited — their decline reduces physical cash access for underserved geographies.
implication: Declining offsite ATMs reduce repayment infrastructure for rural borrowers who remain cash-dependent. Lenders with rural loan books should assess whether offsite ATM coverage in their geographies is contracting — this can impair EMI collection in areas without digital payment penetration.

title: Gap: Top 5 banks hold 93.1% of POS terminals — acquiring market is highly concentrated
body: In Mar 2026, the top 5 banks account for 93.1% of all deployed POS terminals in India. RBL BANK LTD leads at 22.5% market share. All remaining banks combined share less than 6.9% of merchant acquiring infrastructure.
implication: POS concentration at 93.1% in 5 banks means meaningful merchant transaction data for credit underwriting is controlled by a tiny number of institutions. Lenders without data-sharing partnerships with these top POS acquirers are effectively blind to the majority of formal merchant transaction history in India.
